coredata.dc:description: An effective method was proposed for constructing carbon dots (CDs)-sensitized multijunction composite photoelectrodes via one-step cladding a CDs-embedded ZnO amorphous overlayer on vertically aligned metal oxide nanowires. This strategy involved the double role of hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) in the ethylene glycol (EG) solvent mixed with a controllable trace amount of water. In the water-deficient synthetic system, a limited portion of HMTA served as the pH buffer and hydroxyl source to force the hydrolytic process of zinc ions for the production of ZnO. The precipitated ZnO clusters were instantly capped by EG molecules through the activated alkoxidation reaction, and further crosslinked into an amorphous network surrounding the individual nanowires. Meanwhile, the excess HMTA was simultaneously depleted as the precursor for producing CDs in the EG solution through thermal condensation, which were packed in the gradually formed aggregates. We revealed that a CDs-embedded amorphous ZnO overlayer with an appropriate proportion of ingredient could be tailored through an optimal tradeoff between hydrolysis and condensation of HMTA. Benefiting from the synergy of the amorphous ZnO layer and the embedded CDs, the multijunction composite photoanodes exhibited significantly improved PEC performance and stability for water oxidation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the procedure for coating a CDs-embedded ZnO amorphous overlayer on the ZnO NWA aligned on the FTO/glass substrate. SEM images of ZnO (b) and ZnO@C-ZnO (c) by top view; inset is the corresponding cross-sectional view of each NWA sample. (d) TEM image of one ZnO@C-ZnO nanowire; inset is its SAED pattern. (e) HAADF-STEM image of the ZnO@C-ZnO nanowire and corresponding EDX element mappings.

(a) SEM image of the C-ZnO precipitate; inset is the SAED pattern of one single microsphere obtained by TEM. (b) HAADF-STEM image of one C-ZnO microsphere and corresponding EDX element mappings. (c) XRD pattern of the C-ZnO precipitate. (d) UV–vis absorption, PL and PLE spectra of the suspension extracted from the C-ZnO precipitate by acid etching; insets are digital photos of the suspension under ambient light (i) and 365-nm UV light (ii). (e) PL spectra of the suspension with different excitation wavelengths. (f) PL spectrum of the suspension extracted from the ZnO@C-ZnO NWAs by acid etching; inset is a digital photo of the suspension under 365-nm UV light.

(a) XRD patterns of the ZnO and ZnO@C-ZnO NWAs. (b) FT-IR spectra of the ZnO and ZnO@C-ZnO nanowires. (c) XPS element survey spectrum of ZnO@C-ZnO and high-resolution spectra of Zn 2p (d), O 1 s (e), and C 1 s (f).

SEM images of ZnO@C-ZnO-0 (a), ZnO@C-ZnO-2 (b), ZnO@C-ZnO-3 (c), and ZnO@C-ZnO-4 (d); inset is the cross-sectional view of each NWA sample. (e) XRD patterns of the four samples. (f) Normalized UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of ZnO, ZnO@C-ZnO-0, ZnO@C-ZnO-1, ZnO@C-ZnO-2, ZnO@C-ZnO-3, and ZnO@C-ZnO-4.

(a) LSV plots of ZnO, ZnO@C-ZnO-0, ZnO@C-ZnO-1, ZnO@C-ZnO-2, ZnO@C-ZnO-3, and ZnO@C-ZnO-4 under full-spectrum light illumination (100 mW cm−2). (b) Amperometric J–t curves at a bias of 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl under chopped light illumination, (c) measured and fitted M−S plots, and (d) Nyquist plots of the samples (inset of d is the equivalent circuit diagram; inserted table in d lists the calculated values of R ct).

(a) Photocurrent intensity profile of various ZnO NWA samples experiencing 400 s at a bias of 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl under full-spectrum light illumination (100 mW cm−2). (b) Schematic diagram of the ZnO@C-ZnO multijunction NWA photoanode with improved PEC water splitting performance.

Top-view SEM images of TiO2 (a) and TiO2@C-ZnO (b). (c) TEM image of one TiO2@C-ZnO nanowire; inset is its SAED pattern. (d) HRTEM image showing the core–shell interface of the TiO2@C-ZnO nanowire. (e) HAADF-STEM image of one TiO2@C-ZnO nanowire and corresponding EDX element mappings.

(a) LSV plots of TiO2 and TiO2@C-ZnO under full-spectrum light illumination (100 mW cm−2). (b) Amperometric J–t curves at a bias of 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl under chopped light illumination. (c) PEC H2 evolution quantities and the associated Faradic efficiencies measured at 1.23 V vs. RHE in neutral electrolyte under simulated solar light by using TiO2 and TiO2@C-ZnO as photoanode. (d) Photocurrent decay curves of TiO2 and TiO2@C-ZnO in the process of the PEC water splitting reaction.

head.ce:title: One-step cladding metal oxide nanowires with a carbon-dots-embedded ZnO amorphous layer toward boosted photoelectrochemical water oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An effective method was proposed for constructing carbon dots (CDs)-sensitized multijunction composite photoelectrodes via one-step cladding a CDs-embedded ZnO amorphous overlayer on vertically aligned metal oxide nanowires. This strategy involved the double role of hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) in the ethylene glycol (EG) solvent mixed with a controllable trace amount of water. In the water-deficient synthetic system, a limited portion of HMTA served as the pH buffer and hydroxyl source to force the hydrolytic process of zinc ions for the production of ZnO. The precipitated ZnO clusters were instantly capped by EG molecules through the activated alkoxidation reaction, and further crosslinked into an amorphous network surrounding the individual nanowires. Meanwhile, the excess HMTA was simultaneously depleted as the precursor for producing CDs in the EG solution through thermal condensation, which were packed in the gradually formed aggregates. We revealed that a CDs-embedded amorphous ZnO overlayer with an appropriate proportion of ingredient could be tailored through an optimal tradeoff between hydrolysis and condensation of HMTA. Benefiting from the synergy of the amorphous ZnO layer and the embedded CDs, the multijunction composite photoanodes exhibited significantly improved PEC performance and stability for water oxidation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Facing the environmental crisis and the growth of energy demand for solar energy utilization, photoelectrochemical (PEC) and photochemical conversions have been regarded as promising solutions in terms of efficient use of solar energy by converting photons into oxygen and hydrogen liberated from aqueous solution [1–7]. In PEC cells semiconductor nanowire arrays (NWAs) are an attractive photoelectrode configuration attributable to their high specific surface area and fast charge migration rate [8–12]. In practice, photoanodes constructed by wide bandgap transition metal oxides like TiO2 and ZnO NWAs not only present the advantages of convenience in preparation, low cost, and non-toxicity, but also provide a high oxidation potential for boosting PEC water oxidation because of the pinning effect of the valence band (VB) hybridized with a significant portion of the O-2p orbital [9,11]. However, these oxide semiconductors still suffer from the limited visible light responsiveness caused by wide bandgap and/or poor stability due to photocorrosion [11,13]. Besides, the low efficiency of charge separation and the high recombination of electron–hole pairs in single-component semiconductor photoelectrodes usually limit the practical application and efficient utilization of solar energy [14].

Various methods have been developed to construct composite semiconductor NWA photoanodes to expand their applications or to optimize the PEC performance [15–18]. In particular, developing junction structures by means of the core–shell radial configuration is widely adopted in photoanode modification to enhance light absorption, charge transfer efficiency, water molecule adsorption, surface reaction as well as chemical stability of the catalysts [19,20]. Exploiting the alignment of energy bands in the junction region, the built-in electric field is able to facilitate the spatial separation and transport of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, and thus promotes the photoelectric conversion efficiency [17,21,22]. Recently, amorphous inorganic semiconductor has shown its merits in construction of crystalline@amorphous composite photoanodes for their unique electronic structures and reduced grain boundaries caused by intrinsic microstructural disorder [21–25]. It is noteworthy that the low-coordinated species and the disorder effect in the ZnO coating layer have been verified to favor the kinetics of the water-splitting reaction via optimal interfacial charge dynamics and a rapider intermediate exchange behavior [26].

Green nontoxic carbon dots (CDs) as a promising zero-dimensional (0D) photosensitizer have been exploited for modification of semiconductor photoanodes for promoting the charge separation and enhancing the light absorption [16,26–32]. Particularly, the distinctive roles of CDs have been perceived in fine configuration of radial multijunction composite photoanodes with an integrated nanoarchitecture. On the one hand, sensitization by CDs of high charge conductivity and highly negative Fermi level could eliminate the charge barrier in the n–n radial junction region by energy level matching [16]. The reconfiguration of a type-II staggered band alignment provided a facilitated energy gradient, which benefited the charge transfer in the composite. As a result, the PEC performance was significantly improved, especially with a negatively shifted onset potential. On the other hand, the introduction of CDs on the wide bandgap oxide junctions not only enhanced optical absorption of the composite photoanode, but also supplied plentiful tunnels which were favorable for directional charge transfer for the photocatalytic oxygen evolution reaction, leading to a durable PEC activity [26].

The general CDs decoration process is achieved by impregnating the substrate into the solution of pre-synthesized CDs, followed by annealing at high temperatures. For improving the adhesion force of CDs loaded on the electrode, specific design of substrate nanostructure or further coating with a thin protecting layer seemed necessary in some case studies [27,32]. In construction of CDs-sensitized multijunction composite photoanodes, PEC activity improvement was found to strongly depend on the decoration density of CDs and their interaction with the metal oxide shell layer of the radial junctions. However, it is not feasible to further regulate the spatial distribution of CDs merely by depositing an ultrathin overlayer of CDs on the nanowire surface.

In this study, starting from ZnO NWAs grown directly on optically transparent substrates, we proposed a one-step solvothermal method for construction of CDs-sensitized multijunction composite photoanodes by cladding a CDs-embedded ZnO amorphous shell layer on the single-crystalline nanowires. The strategy for the deposition of the CDs-embedded ZnO composite overlayer involved the double role of hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) in the ethylene glycol (EG) solvent mixed with a controllable trace amount of water [33–37]. In this water-deficient synthetic system, a limited portion of HMTA served as the pH buffer and hydroxyl source to force the hydrolytic process of zinc ions for producing ZnO. The capping of EG molecules on the precipitated ZnO clusters could interfere with the subsequent growth process of ZnO, leading to a short-range ordered but long-range disordered amorphous state. In contrast, the superfluous HMTA was simultaneously depleted as the precursor for producing CDs in the EG solution via thermal condensation [37]. We revealed that the construction of a CDs-embedded amorphous ZnO shell layer with an appropriate proportion of ingredient could be achieved through an optimal tradeoff between hydrolysis and condensation of HMTA. Benefiting from the synergy of the amorphous ZnO layer and embedded CDs, the multijunction composite photoanode presented boosted PEC water oxidation performance. Furthermore, we demonstrated the versatility of this simple non-epitaxial cladding approach by using single-crystalline TiO2 NWAs as the starting substrate. The resultant CDs-sensitized multijunction composite photoanode showed much superior PEC performance for water oxidation and the concomitant gaseous hydrogen evolution to pristine TiO2 NWAs under simulated sunlight.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, AR grade), and ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2, EG, AR grade) were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Hexamethylenetetramine (C6H12N4, HMTA, AR grade) was procured from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, AR grade) and ethanol (C2H5OH, LR grade) was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrates (14 Ω) were bought from Wuhan Jinge-Solar Energy Technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses of CDs-modified multijunction composite photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO NWAs were grown on the FTO/glass substrate (30 × 10 × 1.6 mm) through a chemical solution reaction according to a standard procedure [33], in which a ZnO seed layer was coated by atomic layer deposition (ALD). The obtained ZnO NWA electrodes were annealed at 500 °C in air for 1 h. A one-step hydrothermal method was conducted to deposit CDs-embedded amorphous ZnO shell layers on ZnO NWAs. In a typical experiment, 2.5 mmol Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.625 mmol HMTA was dissolved in 100 mL of EG to form a solution, which was further added with 0.6 v% water and stirred for 5 min. A piece of FTO/glass substrate grown with the ZnO NWA was submerged in 15 mL of the precursor solution contained in a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL volume) with the sample side facing down. The sealed autoclave was kept at 170 °C in a convection furnace for 2 h. After the reaction, the obtained sample (named as ZnO@C-ZnO) was thoroughly rinsed with ethanol and deionized water and dried at room temperature. Under the above experimental conditions, different samples were obtained by changing the proportion of precursor, water addition, and reaction time. For the preparations with HMTA to zinc nitrate in a molar ratio of 0:4, 1:4, 2:4, 3:4, and 4:4 in the precursor solution, the amount of HMTA was adjusted and the resultant samples were denoted as ZnO@C-ZnO-0, ZnO@C-ZnO-1, ZnO@C-ZnO-2, ZnO@C-ZnO-3, and ZnO@C-ZnO-4, respectively. The deposition of CDs-embedded amorphous ZnO shell layers on the TiO2 NWAs followed the same procedure except for TiO2 NWAs as a substitute for ZnO NWAs. The prepared sample was denoted as TiO2@C-ZnO.

The surface morphologies of the as-prepared samples were observed by a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800). The microstructure was investigated by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100 plus) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The TEM instrument was equipped with a Gatan CCD camera and an EDX detector (Oxford instrument) for element analysis under the STEM mode. The crystalline phases were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/MAX-2400) with Cu Kα X-ray radiation at 40 kV. Infrared spectra were recorded in the range of 4000 ∼ 400 cm−1 on a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet iS10). X-ray photoelectron spectra were collected on a high-performance X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCALAB 250), where the binding energy was calibrated by setting 284.6 eV as the adventitious carbon in the C 1 s spectrum. Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) measurements were performed on a fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu RF-5301 PC). The diffuse reflection and absorption spectra were measured with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600).

EC and PEC measurements were conducted by a three-electrode system of an electrochemical station (CHI660E, CH Instruments). Na2SO4 solution (0.5 M, 30 mL) was employed as neutral electrolyte (pH = 7). The NWAs grown on the FTO/glass substrate served as working electrode by immersing an active area of 1.5 cm2 in the electrolyte, together with Ag/AgCl as reference electrode (RE) and Pt wire as counter electrode (CE). The photocurrent density–voltage (J–V) curves were measured on the photoanodes by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) under full-spectrum light illumination. Amperometric J–t curves were measured at an applied bias of 1.23 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) under chopped light irradiation. The output intensity of illumination was 100 mW cm−2, which was provided by a 300 W Xe arc lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Trusttech Co., Ltd.). Based on the Nernst equation V RHE = V Ag/AgCl + 0.197 + 0.059 pH, the potential against RHE (V RHE) was calculated from the potential against Ag/AgCl (V Ag/AgCl). Mott–Schottky (M−S) plots were recorded from –1.0 to 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl at 3000 Hz. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz were measured in dark.

In the experiments of PEC water splitting by the TiO2 and TiO2@C-ZnO samples at 1.23 V vs. RHE, gaseous products were collected from a sealed home-made PEC cell containing Na2SO4 solution (0.5 M, 65 mL) under simulated sunlight irradiation (Xe arc lamp incorporated with an AM-1.5 G filter). The amount of H2 generated was analyzed by a gas chromatograph (Shimadzu GC-2014) using N2 as carrier gas.

Fig. 1 a shows the schematic illustration of the procedure for coating a CDs-embedded ZnO amorphous shell layer on the ZnO NWA aligned on the substrate. First, a ZnO seed layer is deposited on the FTO/glass substrate by ALD. Then the ZnO NWA is grown on the substrate via the chemical solution reaction at a temperature of 90 °C. Next, the ZnO NWA is immersed into the solution of HMTA and zinc nitrate, the solvent of which is EG mixed with a trace amount of water. Finally, an amorphous-ZnO/CDs composite shell layer is one-step deposited on the ZnO NWA surface via a solvothermal reaction at 170 °C.

Fig. 1b shows SEM images of the primary ZnO NWA grown on the FTO/glass substrate. The top view reveals a uniform hexagonal appearance with an average diameter of 50 nm. By cross-sectional view the dense NWA is found vertically aligned with a height of 2 µm. Subsequent to the second growth, the average diameter of the obtained ZnO@C-ZnO nanowires increased to ∼130 nm while the height of the NWA remained almost unchanged, as exhibited in Fig. 1c. Notably, the faceted morphology of the primary nanowires evolved into a rough cylinder with a circular cross section, implying a dominant isotropic growth habit of the overlayer. The detailed microstructure of the ZnO@C-ZnO nanowires was further affirmed by TEM. A distinct core–shell radial interface in an individual nanowire can be observed in Fig. 1d. The diameter of the inner nanowire core is ∼50 nm and the thickness of the porous shell is ∼40 nm, consistent with the SEM observation. The selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) in the inset of Fig. 1d reveals the ZnO@C-ZnO nanowire follows the c-oriented elongation of the primary ZnO nanowire. Moreover, the ZnO@C-ZnO nanowire presents dominant single crystallinity though it has a porous morphology with an unusual contrast in the shell layer under electron beam. This result suggests that the C-ZnO overlayer surrounding the ZnO nanowire core mainly contains an amorphous phase. The elemental mappings together with the HAADF-STEM image (Fig. 1e) demonstrate that the Zn, O, and C elements are distributed uniformly in the entire ZnO@C-ZnO nanowire. In view of the radial structure including a carbon-free ZnO nanowire core, the outer shell is enriched with the C element.

For explicit identification of the component in the shell layer of the ZnO@C-ZnO NWAs, we collected the freestanding brown precipitate (denoted as C-ZnO) in the growth solution, which was formed synchronously with the C-ZnO coating on the ZnO NWA by the solvothermal reaction. It is justified that the C-ZnO precipitate owns the same constituents of substance as the C-ZnO overlayer despite the different nucleation and aggregation manners. The SEM image in Fig. 2 a exhibits that the C-ZnO precipitate consists of spherical particles with an average diameter of 1 µm and each microsphere is an aggregated ensemble of interconnected nanoparticles. The inset of Fig. 2a is the SAED pattern of one single microsphere obtained by TEM. The indistinct halos reflect the low crystallinity of the microsphere. The corresponding elemental mappings together with the HAADF-STEM image shown in Fig. 2b indicate that the C-ZnO microsphere consists of the Zn, O and C elements, analogous to the C-ZnO overlayer deposited on the ZnO nanowires. Fig. 2c exhibits the XRD pattern of the precipitate and we only observe a significantly broadened peak centered at ca. 22°. According to our previous study, the signal characteristic of the amorphous phase is derived from diffraction peak overlapping of the ZnO nanoparticles of low crystallinity and the coexistent CDs [37]. We further removed the ZnO component from the collected precipitate by acid etching in dilute HCl solution and obtained a transparent suspension (inset (i) in Fig. 2d). The suspension exhibits strong absorption with a wavelength range less than 450 nm (Fig. 2d), conforming to the absorption feature of CDs converted from HMTA [37]. Meanwhile, the suspension presents an evident blue fluorescence under UV excitation, as demonstrated in Fig. 2d and its inset (ii). The associated photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum in Fig. 2d has a band at 365 nm, agreeing well with the absorption feature. Under the excitation of 365 nm, the maximum emission is achieved at 445 nm, whereas the PL wavelength of the suspension is dependent on the excitation wavelength in the inspected range (Fig. 2e). The above spectral behavior reveals the unique surface states and energy band structure of CDs. From the PL spectrum and digital photo in Fig. 2f, we can observe that the suspension extracted from the ZnO@C-ZnO nanowires by acid etching presents the same light emission property. Fig. S1a, b shows TEM images of the substance in the suspension, in which uniformly dispersed particles with an average size of ∼4.3 nm (Fig. S1c) are observed. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of a single particle in Fig. S1b reveals the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, typical of CDs. This result further substantiates the existence of CDs in the C-ZnO overlayer surrounding the ZnO nanowires.

The XRD patterns of the primary ZnO and ZnO@C-ZnO NWAs are shown in Fig. 3 a. The highly identical signals from the two samples can be well assigned to (002), (101), (102), and (103) diffraction of wurtzite ZnO. This finding indicates that the C-ZnO overlayer grown on the ZnO nanowires is amorphous without new crystal phase introduced. Comparing the FT-IR spectra of the primary ZnO and ZnO@C-ZnO nanowires (Fig. 3b), we can observe a series of enhanced vibration bands in addition to those originating from pure ZnO. The band around 3395 cm−1 comes from stretching vibration of the O–H group; the bands between 1085 and 885 cm−1 are from stretching vibration of C–H, C=O, and C–C bonds, a proportion of which are supposed to be contributed by the surface groups of CDs [30]. Besides, the bands observed around 1580 and 1408 cm−1 correspond to characteristic vibrations of EG. Therefore, a number of EG molecules remain in the C-ZnO overlayer after the solvothermal reaction.

We also analyzed the chemical components of the ZnO@C-ZnO nanowire surface by XPS. The survey spectrum of the ZnO@C-ZnO NWA shown in Fig. 3c demonstrates the existence of the elements Zn, O, and C, the binding energies (BE) of which were further examined by the high-resolution spectra. The Zn 2p core-level XPS spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows the BE peaks at 1021.5 and 1044.5 eV, which can be allocated to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of the Zn–O bond, respectively [38]. The BE peak attributed to the Zn–C bond was not detected, excluding the C element as the dopant in ZnO. According to the fitting curve in Fig. 3e, the deconvoluted O 1 s spectrum includes three components, where the BE peak at 530.1 eV belongs to the lattice oxygen of ZnO; the BE peaks at 531.2 and 532.1 eV correspond to stretching vibration of C=O and C–O bonds individually. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 3f includes three deconvoluted Gaussian curves with peaks located at 284.7, 285.3, and 288.6 eV, which can be assigned to stretching vibrations of C–C, C–O, and C=O bonds [39,40]. The comparatively high BE peak intensities of both O 1 s and C 1 s from the C=O bond suggest enriched oxygenous-type carbon on the ZnO@C-ZnO nanowire surface, which is probably derived from carbonyl functionalities on the surface of the embedded CDs [26]. As expected, the XPS survey spectrum and element high-resolution spectra of the C-ZnO precipitate (Figure S2) exhibit comparable signals to the ZnO@C-ZnO NWA because its chemical component is similar to that of the C-ZnO overlayer surrounding the primary ZnO nanowires.

Our previous report has revealed that CDs-embedded zinc alkoxide microspheres can be fabricated in the solution of HMTA and zinc acetatein the EG solvent mixed with ∼ 0.05 v% water through a similar solvothermal reaction at 170 °C [37]. HMTA is commonly employed as a hydroxyl source and pH buffer in water for the synthesis of ZnO. When the water fraction falls into a very low content, limited HMTA is supposed to participate in the hydrolytic process of Zn2+ ions for producing ZnO. Thus, dissociated Zn2+ ions are mostly depleted by reacting with EG to generate zinc alkoxide and the excess HMTA is simultaneously converted into CDs via thermal condensation in the EG solution at a temperature of 170 °C. Considering the water fraction is up to 0.6 v% in the current system, the hydrolysis of HMTA may be promoted to increase the alkalinity of the solution, and thus to accelerate the forced hydrolysis of Zn2+ ions to produce ZnO. Meanwhile, EG molecules as a capping agent stabilize the generated ZnO oligomers based on Zn–O bonding through the activated alkoxidation path, which initiates a loose network of ZnO with an amorphous nature. For minimizing the overall surface energy, the EG-capped ZnO oligomers are inclined to aggregate into microspheres in the bulk solution and circular cylinders surrounding the preexisting vertically aligned ZnO nanowires. If the added HMTA reagent cannot be timely depleted by the 0.6 v% amount of water, the unhydrolyzed HMTA is carbonized into CDs surface-grafted with hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups, which are simultaneously embedded in the gradually formed aggregates. In such a manner, one-step cladding of the CDs-embedded ZnO amorphous layer on the ZnO NWA surface is realized. Obviously, the fractions of both HMTA and water in the reaction solution are critical in the tradeoff of hydrolysis and condensation of HMTA for the production of CDs/amorphous ZnO composites with an appropriate component ratio.

We systematically investigated the influences of various synthetic parameters on the morphologies of the resultant CDs/amorphous ZnO coating layers on the ZnO NWAs. First, under the condition of 0.6 v% amount of water and 2.5 mmol zinc nitrate in 100 mL of EG, the molar ratio of HMTA to zinc nitrate was adjusted from the preceding1:4 to 0:4, 2:4, 3:4, and 4:4, respectively. The samples obtained after the solvothermal reaction at 170 °C for 2 h were named as ZnO@C-ZnO-0, ZnO@C-ZnO-2, ZnO@C-ZnO-3, and ZnO@C-ZnO-4, whilst the previous ZnO@C-ZnO was denoted as ZnO@C-ZnO-1 for convenience. From the SEM images of ZnO@C-ZnO-0 (Fig. 4 a), we observe that the morphology is basically consistent with that of the starting ZnO nanowires, indicating no C-ZnO overlayer growth occurred when HMTA was not contained in the precursor solution. This finding also implies that forming a zinc alkoxide overlayer by direct alkoxidation of Zn2+ ions with EG was not favored because of the sluggish kinetics. However, the ZnO NWAs easily fell off from the substrate owing to the corrosion of the underlying seed layer. This reaction is attributable to the trace nitric acid produced by the hydrolysis of zinc nitrate that could hardly be neutralized in the absence of HMTA. As expected, there was no precipitation generated in the solution after the solvothermal process. The residual solution showed no fluorescence response under the excitation of 365 nm, implying that the CDs in the C-ZnO composite were derived from the condensation of HMTA. When the molar ratio of HMTA to zinc nitrate reached 2:4, the produced ZnO@C-ZnO-2 consists of nanowires with a significantly enlarged average diameter of ∼350 nm and a coarser appearance (Fig. 4b). When the molar ratio was up to 3:4, the average nanowire diameter of ZnO@C-ZnO-3 is narrowed to ∼230 nm, and plentiful wrinkles are covered on the nanowire surface (Fig. 4c). ZnO@C-ZnO-4 obtained by further increasing the molar ratio to 4:4 presents a similar morphological feature to ZnO@C-ZnO-3 except that the more wrinkled surface made the nanowires thicker (∼280 nm), as shown in Fig. 4d. The above results demonstrate that HMTA is indispensable to trigger the growth of the overlayer around the primary ZnO nanowires. A high concentration of HMTA favors an adequate alkaline environment for forced hydrolysis of Zn2+ ions as well as self-condensation for the generation of CDs, which is supposed to induce a high growth rate of the overlayer. Nevertheless, if the solution alkalinity was excessively high, the evolution of ZnO easily got close to the growth–dissolution equilibrium with the rapid depletion of Zn2+ ions. The residual alkali caused the etching of ZnO in the overlayer, thinning the shell thickness. Meanwhile, gradual reprecipitation of ZnO created wrinkles on the nanowire surface, which conversely increased the size of the integrated nanostructure.

The XRD patterns of the above samples are exhibited in Fig. 4e. Main signals of each sample are from the diffraction of wurtzite ZnO, similar to those of ZnO@C-ZnO-1 displayed in Fig. 3a. Therefore, no new crystal phase was formed by adjusting the precursor proportion in the secondary growth. We also observe that the peak width of ZnO-(002) diffraction peak centered at 34.2° became broadened with the content of HMTA increased. The asymmetrical peak pattern demonstrates the signal overlapping of the high-crystalline ZnO nanowire core and low-crystalline ZnO overlayer. Fig. 4f summarizes the normalized diffused reflectance UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnO, ZnO@C-ZnO-0, ZnO@C-ZnO-1, ZnO@C-ZnO-2, ZnO@C-ZnO-3, and ZnO@C-ZnO-4 over a wavelength range from 300 to 800 nm. All the samples give strong absorption in the UV range because of the wide bandgap of wurtzite ZnO. Compared to the uncoated ZnO NWAs, only slight light-harvesting enhancements are observed in the visible region for the composite samples. Obviously, the amorphous overlayer does not introduce additional energy levels to modify the electronic structure of ZnO. Fig. 2d has revealed that the embedded CDs cannot significantly promote the visible light absorption, either. Based on the morphological investigation presented before, the light absorption is expected to be enhanced through multiple reflections on rougher surfaces [41]. Thus, the light-trapping effect might be responsible for the improved visible-light absorption of the core–shell NWAs, which seems most effective for ZnO@C-ZnO-1.

Next, we investigated the effect of reaction time on the overlayer growth by fixing the molar ratio of HMTA to zinc nitrate at 1:4 (0.625 mmol HMTA and 2.5 mmol zinc nitrate) and 0.6 v% of water content in 100 mL of EG. The SEM images in Figure S3 illustrate the time-dependent morphological evolution of ZnO NWAs via secondary growth at 170 °C. With the reaction time prolonged, the average nanowire diameter is enlarged stepwise because the overlayer grows thicker. After reaction for 5 h, the nanowires possess a rough side surface and an average diameter up to 400 nm. Coalescent coating of several adjacent nanowires also occurred. The well-developed cylindricalmorphology reflects an isotropic cladding of an amorphous network around the primary ZnO nanowires. When we adjusted the additive amount of water, the morphologies of the products also regularly changed after secondary growth at 170 °C for a constant duration of 2 h. As verified in Figure S4, water is necessary to trigger the growth of the overlayer, and the size evolution of the modified nanowires is sensitive to the water content. Although the average diameter gradually increases from 70 nm to 150 nm by varying the water fraction from 0.2 v% to 1 v%, the nanowires became faceted when the water fraction reached 1 v%, presenting a radially epitaxial growth manner. Therefore, once sufficient water was present in the precursor solution, the rapid release of hydroxide groups from the HMTA would stimulate the forced hydrolysis of dissociative Zn2+ ions to form ZnO monomers. Consequently, the hydrolysis-induced heterogeneous nucleation and epitaxial growth of ZnO dominated the secondary growth on the primary ZnO NWAs. The possible competing reactions including EG-related alkoxidation and HMTA-related condensation were significantly restricted due to the fast depletion of Zn2+ ions and HMTA. Liberated from the disturbances by capping of EG and embedding of CDs, a close-packed ZnO overlayer was finally developed on the nanowire surface.

For revealing the effects of the component and microstructure of the C-ZnO overlayer on the PEC water oxidation of the radial junction NWAs, PEC measurements were conducted by implementing the diverse ZnO-based NWAs as photoanodes [42,43]. Fig. 5 a exhibits the linear sweep voltammograms (LSVs) of the primary ZnO, ZnO@C-ZnO-0, ZnO@C-ZnO-1, ZnO@C-ZnO-2, ZnO@C-ZnO-3 and ZnO@C-ZnO-4 NWAs recorded from –1.0 V to 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl under full-spectrum light illumination. Relatively negative onset potentials and higher photocurrent densities indicate that the PEC water oxidation activities of the junction NWAs are better than the uncoated ZnO NWAs. Therein, the ZnO@C-ZnO-1 NWA achieves the highest photocurrent density of 2.5 mA cm−2 at a bias of 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl, nearly 2.5 times that of the primary ZnO NWA. We subsequently examined the transient photocurrent densities of the above photoelectrodes to investigate the PEC water oxidation performance and photostability under full-spectrum light illumination. The amperometric J–t curves at a bias of 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl (Fig. 5b) show that each photoelectrode displays instantaneous and repeated photocurrent responses to light illumination. The ZnO@C-ZnO-1 NWA not only presents the highest steady-state photocurrent density in every on–off cycle, but also the best stability under continuous illumination. In contrast, the photocurrent densities obtained from the uncoated ZnO NWAs significantly decayed with time due to the severe photocorrosion by the accumulated holes. In light of the improved photoactivity and photostability for all the samples modified with the C-ZnO overlayer, the amorphous ZnO shell and the embedded CDs are supposed to synergistically promote the charge separation of the composites, accelerate the consumption of the photoinduced holes in water oxidation, and thus to relieve the photocorrosion of ZnO.

M−S analyses were performed in the dark to learn the space charge capacitance of the above samples. According to the plots shown in Fig. 5c, the positive slope of the straight linear section suggests the n-type conductance of each sample. The determined flat-band potentials of the ZnO@C-ZnO NWAs (e.g. –0.059 V vs. Ag/AgCl for ZnO@C-ZnO-1) are positively shifted compared to the primary ZnO NWA (–0.088 V vs. Ag/AgCl). This change originates from the more positive Fermi level of the amorphous ZnO in the overlayer, which is equilibrated with that of the crystalline ZnO nanowires by forming n–n junction. The lowest slope of ZnO@C-ZnO-1 indicates the highest carrier density (N d) [44]. The calculated N d of the pure ZnO is 6.27 × 1019 cm−3 while the value of ZnO@C-ZnO-1 is the highest with an increase to 5.37 × 1020 cm−3. The increased N d is likely ascribed to improved charge transport of the optimized multijunction that contains CDs with excellent conductivity [16,26]. EIS measurements were performed in dark for evaluating the charge transfer resistance at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface. The observed semicircles at the low frequency in the Nyquist plots (Fig. 5d) reflect the charge transfer capacitance in the electrodes [45–47]. The equivalent circuit in the inset represents a simple Randles circuit including the resistance of R s and R ct. The fitting resistance R ct in the inserted table indicates that the R ct value significantly reduces after the coating of the C-ZnO composite overlayer and the value obtained from ZnO@C-ZnO-1 is smallest. The favorable inner charge transfer kinetics of ZnO@C-ZnO-1 agrees well with its high N d estimated by the M−S plot, which is closely associated with its largest photocurrent density as evidenced in Fig. 5a.

The photocurrent responses of the control samples described in Figures S3 and S4 are shown in Figures S5 and S6, respectively. The results indicate that the PEC performance is also dependant on appropriate reaction time and water addition at the given molar ratio of HMTA to zinc nitrate in the precursor solution. Based on the measured values listed in Table S1, the variation trend of steady photocurrent for all the investigated samples is illustrated in Fig. 6 a. The intensity profile implies that the maximum photocurrent density experiencing 400 s at a bias of 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl can be achieved on the reaction condition which corresponds to 1:4 M ratio of HMTA to zinc nitrate, 0.6 v% of water addition and 2 h of growth time. Obviously, the above synthetic parameters can satisfy the tradeoff between hydrolysis and condensation of HMTA during the reaction process, which determines the optimal portions of amorphous ZnO and CDs in the C-ZnO overlayer.

To understand the synergy of the amorphous ZnO shell and the embedded CDs on the outstanding PEC performance of ZnO@C-ZnO-1, we compared its PL spectrum to that of the primary ZnO NWA. As shown in Figure S7, no emission in the 400–600 nm range was found for the CDs embedded in the amorphous ZnO under the excitation of 365 nm, differing from the PL property of the CDs extracted from the C-ZnO overlayer (Fig. 2f). Therefore, the relaxation process of the CDs anchored in ZnO@C-ZnO-1 is mainly in the non-radiative form under irradiation. This finding demonstrates the pronounced charge-separation characteristics of the embedded CDs by forming junctions with the surrounding ZnO matrix.

Fig. 6b illustrates a proposed schematic diagram of the band configuration and the interfacial charge separation mechanism of the ZnO@C-ZnO multijunction photoanode. Although ZnO is n-type in both the crystalline and amorphous states, the Fermi level of single-crystalline ZnO nanowires is closer to the CB. When the two phases are intimately contacted, the electrons will flow from the ZnO nanowire to the amorphous ZnO overlayer for equilibrating the Fermi levels, forming n–n homojunction at the radial interface. Under the light irradiation, the built-in electric field directing from the core to the shell in the depletion region can facilitate the separation of photogenerated holes and electrons. Meanwhile, the LUMO energy level of the embedded CDs is more negative, so the electrons of the CDs can migrate to the CB of the amorphous ZnO and further to the ZnO nanowire core along the energy level gradient, leading to the increase of carrier concentration. Accordingly, the VB of the amorphous ZnO and the HOMO energy level of the CDs present a stepwise arrangement to that of the ZnO nanowire, which favors the synchronous transport of the holes in the opposite direction. Such an unimpeded energy transfer pathway constructed inside the multijunction can reduce the recombination probability of the photogenerated charge carriers. Notably, carboxyl-conjugated CDs have been verified to supply unique tunnels for directional charge transfer of the holes in water oxidation [26]. The high dispersion of CDs in the amorphous ZnO overlayer greatly increases the spatial distribution of CDs, which enables the creation of plentiful favorable tunnels for mediating interfacial charge dynamics. Besides, the porous structure of the C-ZnO overlayer can enlarge the area of the photoanode contacted with the electrolyte, thus shortening the migration distance of the holes to the reaction front. The rapid consumption of the separated holes in water oxidation further hinders the accumulation-induced recombination process [22,48]. With more separated electrons flowing from the ZnO nanowire core to the external circuit, an enhanced photocurrent density is achieved. Obviously, the overall PEC performance involving photocatalytic activity, photostability and structural stability of the composite photoanode with an integrated nanoarchitecture is drastically affected by the ratio of constituents, thickness and porosity of the overlayer, which can be tailored by an optimal tradeoff between hydrolysis and condensation of HMTA during the reaction process.

In a control experiment, ZnO@C-ZnO-1 was annealed at 550 °C in air for 1 h (denoted as ZnO@C-ZnO-a) to further clarify the role of the composite overlayer in the enhanced PEC performance. After etching the ZnO@C-ZnO-a sample in dilute HCl solution, we observed that the fluorescence emission of the extracted suspension obviously attenuated after annealing (inset of Figure S8), indicating the partial loss of CDs that suffered from oxidation. Although heat treatment at 550 °C resulted in the conversion of amorphous ZnO in the overlayer to polycrystalline ZnO with fewer defects, the photocurrent densities of ZnO@C-ZnO-a noticeably decreased in the whole scan region and the value obtained at each bias almost returned to that of the primary ZnO NWA (Figure S8). The above result further reveals the synergy between the amorphous ZnO and the embedded CDs in the ZnO@C-ZnO multijunction on charge carrier mobility and photocatalytic activity for the boosted PEC water oxidation.

On the basis of the non-epitaxial isotropic growth habit of the C-ZnO overlayer, we extended this synthetic strategy to construct radial multijunction for TiO2 nanowires that are rich in surface hydroxyl groups. The universal applicability of this one-step cladding method was investigated by replacing the ZnO NWA with the TiO2 NWA under the predetermined optimum solvothermal condition. Fig. 7 a, b shows SEM top-views of the TiO2 NWA and TiO2@C-ZnO NWA vertically aligned on the FTO/glass substrate, respectively. The unobvious changes in size and shape of the modified nanowires indicate a relatively low growth rate of the overlayer on the TiO2 nanowire surface. The TEM image in Fig. 7c confirms the presence of a uniform thin overlayer on the TiO2@C-ZnO nanowire. As expected, the SAED pattern (inset) merely shows the typical diffraction spots derived from single crystalline structure of rutile-phase TiO2 considering the amorphous feature of the C-ZnO overlayer. The HRTEM image (Fig. 7d) of the TiO2@C-ZnO nanowire exhibits a distinct crystalline/amorphous core–shell interface with a lattice spacing of 0.33 nm in the crystalline phase, corresponding to the (110) plane of the tetragonal rutile phase TiO2. The thickness of the intimately contacted amorphous overlayer is only ∼5 nm, consistent with the SEM observation. As depicted in Fig. 7d, the element mappings reveal the uniform distributions of Ti, O, Zn, and C in the entire TiO2@C-ZnO nanowire. Notably, the EDS signal widths of Zn and C are larger than that of Ti, which verifies the cladding of the C-ZnO overlayer on the TiO2 nanowire. Therefore, the TiO2@C-ZnO photoanode with an integrated nanoarchitecture was successfully prepared by this one-step solvothermal method.

The LSV curves in Fig. 8 a demonstrate that the photocurrent density of TiO2@C-ZnO is significantly improved with respect to the primary TiO2 NWA under full-spectrum illumination with a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2. The TiO2@C-ZnO sample displays an instantaneous and repeated photocurrent response with a high photocurrent density of ∼5.0 mA cm−2 at a bias of 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl under chopped light illumination (Fig. 8b). Finally, we applied TiO2@C-ZnO to PEC water splitting for the production of hydrogen under simulated solar light. The evolution of H2 gas versus time was investigated by running the photoanode in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution at 1.23 V vs. RHE. A linear increase of H2 amount was achieved under the operation condition (Fig. 8c). The H2 evolution rate in virtue of TiO2@C-ZnO reaches 57.2 μmol h−1 cm−2, which is about 5.7 times of the pristine TiO2 production rate. This result also demonstrates a prominent PEC H2 production efficiency among different CDs-modified TiO2-based heterojunction/multijunction photoanodes, especially in neutral electrolyte solution (Table S2). In addition, TiO2@C-ZnO exhibited excellent stability during the long-time hydrogen production process. As shown in Fig. 8d, the photocurrent density only slightly decayed by about 0.3 mA cm−2 after the cyclic water splitting test for 4 h. The calculated Faradic efficiency of the TiO2@C-ZnO photoanode is about 86.38%, which is higher than 74.43% of the primary TiO2 NWA. The less loss may be associated with the synergistic effect of the C-ZnO overlayer in promoting the separation, transfer, and targeted reaction of photoexcited holes in the composite multijunction for PEC water oxidation.

In summary, we successfully deposited a CDs-embedded ZnO amorphous shell layer on single-crystalline ZnO nanowires by a one-step solvothermal reaction for the construction of CDs-sensitized multijunction composite photoanodes with an integrated nanoarchitecture. Different from the CDs decoration processes reported in literature [16,26,27], the distribution density of CDs could be finely adjusted in the amorphous ZnO overlayer by varying the synthetic parameters based on the tradeoff between hydrolysis and condensation of HMTA. The introduction of CDs supplied many favorable tunnels for directional charge transfer, while the built-in electric field at the radial junction interface facilitated the separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. The CDs-sensitized multijunction composite photoanode prepared under the optimal condition demonstrated a high steady photocurrent density and significantly improved stability. By cladding TiO2 nanowires with the CDs-embedded amorphous ZnO overlayer, the resultant multijunction composite photoanode provided a PEC hydrogen production rate of 57.2 μmol h−1 cm−2 under the simulated sunlight, drastically outperforming the primary TiO2 nanowire photoanode. Our work offers a simple and versatile strategy for fabricating high-performance CDs-related multijunction composite photoelectrodes in solar energy conversion.
